summary: Gruber previews WWDC 2014, covering the Beats acquisition, Mac OS X 10.10's visual overhaul, iOS 8, hardware expectations, and iCloud photography. He argues the Beats deal is straightforward despite seeming unusual for Apple, and expresses most excitement about Mac OS X 10.10's redesign. He predicts a stark iOS 7-inspired look, a new system font replacing Lucida Grande, and a cleanup of the confusing spatial layout of Mission Control/Spaces/Launchpad. On iCloud, he makes a strong case for unlimited photo storage and a ground-up iPhoto rewrite as a cloud-centric photo library, while suggesting Apple should retire Aperture.
key_points: The Beats acquisition is straightforward: Apple wanted a streaming service, Jimmy Iovine's industry connections, and a profitable hardware business with global expansion potential

Mac OS X 10.10 is getting an iOS 7-style visual overhaul, likely including a new system font (replacing Lucida Grande for the first time since 2001) and a desperately needed cleanup of the confusing Mission Control/Spaces/Launchpad spatial model
Apple has quietly become one of the most important camera companies in the world, and digital photo management remains an unsolved problem requiring an iCloud-centric rewrite of iPhoto
iOS is Apple's primary platform with an order of magnitude more users than Mac OS X, yet iOS 8 details remain thin beyond Healthbook
No new hardware is expected at WWDC, with the busy second half of 2014 reserved for hardware introductions
money_quotes: I'd rather use an iPhone that can't make phone calls than use one with a broken camera.
Mission Control, Spaces, Launchpad, Dashboard — where are these things? How do you get to them? It's all a confusing jumble of ideas that have been glommed together piece by piece over 15 years.
My concern isn't that Beats is a bad fit for Apple, but rather that it might be a sign of lessening focus.
The iTunes desktop app runs on Windows, but that's an historical anomaly at this point — the iWhatever brands are of Apple, by Apple, and for Apple users.
Digital photo management remains an unsolved problem. What are we supposed to do when our iOS devices run out of space because of all the photos we're storing on them?
Apple's solution is from the Mac-as-digital-hub era: plug your iPhone or iPad to your Mac and import your photos into iPhoto. That feels antediluvian today.

Somehow I doubt that the same company that launched last year's WWDC with the splendid 'A Thousand No's for Every Yes' animated video has decided to change their ratio of no's to yes's in just one year.
key_insight: Apple's most urgent need isn't flashy new hardware but solving the unsolved problem of cloud-centric photo management, while Mac OS X desperately needs both a visual and conceptual overhaul to clean up 15 years of accumulated spatial confusion.
